refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable operators and the system to achieve and maintain energy efficiency performance such that measured energy_per_ton (energy_used / ton_moved) does not exceed the customer-defined E_target over the agreed evaluation period, under nominal operating conditions and across the range of supported worksite scenarios.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-003 as stated is a KPI support requirement but lacks specificity on measurement scope, boundary conditions, and operational context. Refinement clarifies that energy efficiency is a measurable outcome dependent on operator behavior, vehicle efficiency, and system-level optimization. The requirement now explicitly bounds the evaluation period and scenario scope, making it verifiable and traceable to design decisions.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Establish baseline energy consumption and ton-moved metrics during acceptance testing across representative worksite scenarios (e.g., load types, terrain, operator skill levels). Compare measured energy_per_ton against E_target. Require telemetry capture and post-analysis to isolate vehicle efficiency, operational losses, and system-level optimization contributions.
sub_frs: FR-EE-001: The vehicle shall minimize energy consumption per unit load moved through efficient drivetrain, hydraulic, and powertrain design.

FR-EE-002: The teleoperation system shall provide operator feedback and control responsiveness to enable efficient task execution without energy waste due to control latency or poor situational awareness.
FR-EE-003: The NGHE solution shall collect, aggregate, and report energy consumption and productivity metrics (ton_moved) to enable performance tracking against E_target.
FR-EE-004: Safety services shall not impose energy penalties that degrade efficiency below E_target through unnecessary operational constraints or redundant actuation.

candidate_dps: DP-EE-A: Optimize vehicle powertrain efficiency (engine, transmission, hydraulics) and implement real-time energy monitoring with operator feedback loops to guide efficient operation.

DP-EE-B: Implement a closed-loop energy management system that monitors energy consumption, predicts efficiency degradation, and automatically adjusts operational parameters (load distribution, speed, duty cycle) to maintain energy_per_ton ≤ E_target.

DP-EE-C: Combine vehicle-level efficiency optimization (DP-EE-A) with system-level telemetry aggregation and operator coaching via teleoperation interface to achieve target through design + operational discipline.

DP-EE-D: Establish energy efficiency as a constraint in the tele-operator task planning and execution layer, with automated task decomposition to minimize energy waste and real-time energy budget enforcement.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-EE-C: Combine vehicle-level efficiency optimization (powertrain, hydraulics, drivetrain design) with system-level telemetry aggregation (energy consumption, ton_moved tracking) and operator coaching via teleoperation interface to achieve energy_per_ton ≤ E_target through integrated design and operational discipline.

potential_sources: Vehicle powertrain efficiency is decoupled from teleoperation control latency; both contribute independently to energy_per_ton.
Telemetry aggregation (IM: NGHE Solution) depends on Vehicle telemetry output (IM: Vehicle) but does not couple design decisions.
Operator coaching via teleoperation does not require closed-loop automatic control, reducing coupling complexity and safety risk.
coupling_check.notes: DP-EE-C avoids tight coupling of automatic energy management (DP-EE-B) which would require safety-critical control loops and increase verification burden. Instead, it distributes responsibility: Vehicle provides efficiency; System provides visibility; Operator provides discipline. This aligns with high safety criticality by keeping human operators in the decision loop.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Operator will respond to energy efficiency feedback and coaching to maintain discipline. Risk: Operator behavior variability may prevent achievement of E_target despite optimal vehicle design. Mitigation: Teleoperation interface must provide clear, actionable feedback; acceptance testing must include operator training and skill assessment.

Assumption: Vehicle powertrain efficiency improvements are achievable within cost and weight constraints. Risk: Efficiency gains may be marginal or require expensive components. Mitigation: Conduct powertrain trade studies early; establish efficiency baseline and target decomposition.

Assumption: Telemetry collection and aggregation do not introduce significant energy overhead. Risk: Sensor and communication power consumption could offset efficiency gains. Mitigation: Quantify telemetry power budget; optimize sensor sampling rates.

Assumption: E_target is achievable under nominal conditions. Risk: Target may be unrealistic given worksite variability. Mitigation: Validate E_target against historical data and competitor benchmarks during requirements phase.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-EE-C to explicitly specify: (1) vehicle powertrain efficiency targets (e.g., fuel consumption per kW output), (2) telemetry metrics to be collected and aggregation frequency, (3) operator coaching mechanism (e.g., real-time energy budget display, efficiency score), and (4) acceptance test protocol including operator skill levels and worksite scenario definitions.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: System-level integration of vehicle, teleoperation, and safety services. Responsible for energy efficiency KPI realization through: (1) aggregation of vehicle telemetry (energy consumption, ton_moved), (2) computation and reporting of energy_per_ton metric, (3) operator coaching interface via teleoperation, and (4) coordination with safety services to ensure efficiency targets do not conflict with safety constraints.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle provides energy consumption (kWh or fuel volume), ton_moved (load mass × distance), operational state (idle, active, fault). Frequency: 1 Hz or event-driven. Used by NGHE Solution to compute energy_per_ton and detect efficiency anomalies.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Site Manager or tele-operator provides task directives (load, destination, priority). NGHE Solution uses task context to set energy efficiency expectations and provide operator coaching relative to task-specific baselines.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Safety Services provides safety constraints and alerts. NGHE Solution must verify that energy efficiency optimizations do not violate safety constraints. If conflict detected, NGHE Solution escalates to Safety Services for resolution.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution communicates energy efficiency metrics, operator coaching, and energy budget status to tele-operator and Site Manager via Network Link. Also receives updated E_target or operational policies from Site Manager.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural system-level integrator. It owns energy efficiency KPI computation, operator coaching, and cross-module coordination. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry aggregation logic and metric computation are contained here. Vehicle and Teleoperation modules provide inputs but do not own efficiency logic.

Vehicle owns powertrain efficiency design (DP-EE-C component 1) and telemetry emission. However, energy_per_ton computation and operator coaching are system-level concerns, not vehicle-local. If efficiency logic were distributed to Vehicle, it would create diffusion across Vehicle and Teleoperation modules.

Teleoperation owns operator coaching interface and control responsiveness (DP-EE-C component 2). However, energy_per_ton metric computation and cross-module coordination belong to NGHE Solution. Assigning efficiency KPI ownership to Teleoperation would couple it tightly to Vehicle telemetry and Safety Services, increasing diffusion.

Safety Services is not a natural owner of energy efficiency KPI. Its role is to ensure efficiency optimizations do not compromise safety. Assigning efficiency ownership to Safety Services would create high diffusion and conflate safety and efficiency concerns.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: NGHE Solution is the appropriate IM boundary because: (1) it is the system-level integrator responsible for cross-module coordination, (2) energy_per_ton is a system-level KPI that depends on Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services inputs, (3) operator coaching and efficiency feedback are system-level concerns that require aggregation of multiple data sources, and (4) assigning ownership to NGHE Solution minimizes diffusion and maintains clear separation of concerns (Vehicle: powertrain efficiency; Teleoperation: operator interface; Safety Services: safety constraints).
containment_risks: Risk: NGHE Solution may become a 'god module' if too many cross-cutting concerns are assigned to it. Mitigation: Clearly define NGHE Solution scope as system integration and KPI computation only; delegate powertrain efficiency design to Vehicle and operator interface design to Teleoperation.

Risk: Telemetry aggregation and energy_per_ton computation may introduce latency or data loss, affecting real-time operator coaching. Mitigation: Specify telemetry collection frequency, aggregation latency budget, and fallback behavior if telemetry is unavailable.

Risk: Operator coaching logic may be complex and difficult to verify if embedded in NGHE Solution. Mitigation: Separate coaching logic into a well-defined module with clear inputs (telemetry, task context) and outputs (coaching message, energy budget status).

Risk: Safety Services may impose constraints that make E_target unachievable. Mitigation: Establish early coordination between NGHE Solution and Safety Services to validate that efficiency and safety targets are compatible.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Refine NGHE Solution IM boundary to explicitly include: (1) Telemetry Aggregation submodule (collects and buffers vehicle telemetry), (2) Energy KPI Computation submodule (computes energy_per_ton, detects anomalies), (3) Operator Coaching submodule (generates coaching messages and energy budget status), and (4) Safety Coordination submodule (validates efficiency optimizations against safety constraints). Define clear interfaces between these submodules and external modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services).

fr_dp_gate.reason: Refined FR-EE (energy efficiency KPI) is clearly mapped to DP-EE-C (vehicle efficiency + system telemetry + operator coaching). The DP addresses all sub-FRs: FR-EE-001 (vehicle efficiency) via powertrain optimization, FR-EE-002 (teleoperation feedback) via operator coaching interface, FR-EE-003 (metric reporting) via telemetry aggregation, and FR-EE-004 (safety compatibility) via safety coordination. No independence violations detected; DP-EE-C is a coherent solution concept.

diffusion_risks: Telemetry aggregation logic is centralized in NGHE Solution, reducing diffusion. However, if operator coaching logic becomes complex, it may need to be distributed across Teleoperation (UI) and NGHE Solution (logic), creating diffusion. Mitigation: Keep coaching logic in NGHE Solution; Teleoperation only renders UI.

Vehicle powertrain efficiency design is owned by Vehicle module; NGHE Solution only consumes telemetry. This is clean separation. However, if Vehicle needs to adjust efficiency parameters based on real-time feedback from NGHE Solution, coupling may increase. Mitigation: Define clear feedback loop protocol (e.g., energy budget adjustment commands) to avoid ad-hoc coupling.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate IM boundary for energy efficiency KPI ownership. Containment score of 0.95 indicates minimal diffusion. Vehicle and Teleoperation modules are well-scoped to their respective concerns (powertrain efficiency and operator interface). Safety Services is appropriately positioned as a constraint provider, not an efficiency owner.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Refined FR-EE is verifiable through acceptance testing: (1) measure energy_per_ton during representative worksite scenarios, (2) compare against E_target, (3) validate that telemetry collection and operator coaching do not introduce unacceptable latency or energy overhead. Verification is feasible if E_target is realistic and acceptance test protocol is well-defined.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation of energy efficiency responsibility is clear: Vehicle provides powertrain efficiency; Teleoperation provides operator interface and feedback; NGHE Solution provides KPI computation and cross-module coordination; Safety Services provides safety constraints. Each module has a well-defined role and interface. Ownership of NGHE Solution is appropriate for system-level KPI realization.

recommended_revisions: Refine acceptance test protocol to specify: (1) worksite scenarios (load types, terrain, operator skill levels), (2) measurement period and data collection frequency, (3) baseline energy_per_ton and E_target values, (4) operator training and skill assessment, (5) post-test analysis methodology to isolate vehicle efficiency, operational losses, and system-level optimization contributions.

Define telemetry collection and aggregation requirements: (1) energy consumption measurement accuracy and frequency, (2) ton_moved calculation method (load mass × distance or direct measurement), (3) aggregation latency budget for real-time operator coaching, (4) fallback behavior if telemetry is unavailable.

Establish safety-efficiency coordination protocol: (1) identify potential conflicts between safety constraints and efficiency targets, (2) define escalation procedure if conflict is detected, (3) validate that E_target is achievable under all safety constraints.

Refine operator coaching mechanism: (1) specify coaching message content and frequency, (2) define energy budget display format and update rate, (3) validate that coaching does not distract operator or compromise safety.

decision.justification: Refined FR-EE and selected DP-EE-C are well-formed and appropriately assigned to NGHE Solution IM boundary. However, the requirement is not atomic and has been decomposed into four sub-FRs (FR-EE-001 through FR-EE-004). Each sub-FR requires further decomposition to define detailed design parameters and implementation modules. Additionally, critical details (telemetry collection, operator coaching, safety coordination) must be refined at the next level to ensure feasibility and verify that E_target is achievable. Proceeding to next level decomposition is necessary to establish detailed design and implementation strategy.
decision.justification_detail: The current level establishes the system-level energy efficiency strategy (vehicle efficiency + telemetry + operator coaching). The next level must decompose each sub-FR into detailed DPs and IMs: (1) FR-EE-001 requires powertrain efficiency trade studies and Vehicle module design, (2) FR-EE-002 requires teleoperation interface design and control latency analysis, (3) FR-EE-003 requires telemetry architecture and KPI computation logic, (4) FR-EE-004 requires safety-efficiency coordination protocol. Proceeding to next level will establish these details and enable verification planning.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-EE-001: The vehicle shall minimize energy consumption per unit load moved through efficient drivetrain, hydraulic, and powertrain design.

FR-EE-002: The teleoperation system shall provide operator feedback and control responsiveness to enable efficient task execution without energy waste due to control latency or poor situational awareness.
FR-EE-003: The NGHE solution shall collect, aggregate, and report energy consumption and productivity metrics (ton_moved) to enable performance tracking against E_target.
FR-EE-004: Safety services shall not impose energy penalties that degrade efficiency below E_target through unnecessary operational constraints or redundant actuation.

assumptions: Operator will respond to energy efficiency feedback and coaching to maintain discipline.
Vehicle powertrain efficiency improvements are achievable within cost and weight constraints.
Telemetry collection and aggregation do not introduce significant energy overhead.
E_target is achievable under nominal conditions and across supported worksite scenarios.
Safety constraints and efficiency targets are compatible; no fundamental conflicts exist.
Telemetry collection frequency of 1 Hz is sufficient for real-time operator coaching and post-test analysis.
Operator skill level and training are adequate to achieve E_target; acceptance testing will include skill assessment.
open_risks: Operator behavior variability may prevent achievement of E_target despite optimal vehicle design.
Vehicle powertrain efficiency gains may be marginal or require expensive components.
Telemetry power consumption could offset efficiency gains.
E_target may be unrealistic given worksite variability.
Safety constraints may impose operational limitations that make E_target unachievable.
Telemetry aggregation latency may degrade real-time operator coaching effectiveness.
Operator coaching logic may become complex and difficult to verify.
NGHE Solution may become a 'god module' if too many cross-cutting concerns are assigned to it.
evidence_needed: Powertrain efficiency baseline and target decomposition (e.g., fuel consumption per kW output).
Historical energy_per_ton data from comparable equipment to validate E_target realism.
Telemetry architecture design and power budget analysis.
Operator coaching interface mockup and usability testing results.
Safety-efficiency coordination protocol and conflict resolution procedure.
Acceptance test protocol with worksite scenarios, measurement methodology, and operator skill assessment.
Vehicle module design trade studies (powertrain, hydraulics, drivetrain efficiency).
Teleoperation module design (control latency, feedback responsiveness, operator interface).
NGHE Solution module design (telemetry aggregation, KPI computation, operator coaching, safety coordination).
